---
# ── FILL IN YOUR DETAILS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
title: "Your Post Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2025-04-15"                         # YYYY-MM-DD format
description: "One sentence. Make it good." # Appears in the post listing
categories: [meditation]                   # Replace with your topic
image: "images/cover.jpg"                  # Optional — delete this line if no image
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Uncomment the two lines below once you have a references.bib file:
# bibliography: references.bib

toc: true
---

<!-- DELETE THIS BLOCK before submitting ──────────────────────────────────────
MVP TEMPLATE — just the essentials

This is all you need:
  - A clear title and author
  - 800–1000 words of science communication (written in the third person)
  - At least one original visual aid you created (not taken from the article)
  - In-text citations
  - A full APA reference list at the end

Suggested structure:
  1. Opening hook — a surprising finding or question that draws the reader in
  2. Context — what is this area of research about?
  3. The study — what did the researchers do, and what did they find?
  4. What it means — why does this matter to a non-expert reader?
  5. References

Write as if explaining to a curious, intelligent friend who has not studied psychology.
Do not start with "In this post I will...". Do not use jargon without explaining it.
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->


## Your opening

Start with something that pulls the reader in. A surprising finding, a counter-intuitive claim, a question worth asking.

Write naturally, in the third person. Describe what the researchers found — not what "we" found.


## Background

What is this area of research about? Give the reader enough context to understand why the study matters.

Cite your sources inline: [@authorYear] or [@firstAuthor2020; @secondAuthor2021].


## The study

Describe the specific article you chose. What was the research question? How was the study designed? What did the researchers find?

Keep it concrete. Avoid passive constructions like "it was found that". Instead: "Smith and colleagues found that..."


## What does this mean?

Connect the findings to the real world. Why should a non-expert care about this research?

![A clear, descriptive caption explaining what this figure shows. Replace this with your own visual.](images/your-figure.png)


## References

::: {#refs}
:::

---

::: {.author-card}

<div>
<div class="author-card-name">Your Name</div>
<div class="author-card-bio">
  Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London.
  Write one sentence about your interests here.
</div>
</div>
:::

*This post is published under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).*
